data.title: Global Ship Lease: Combining Value And Growth
data.description:
Global Ship Lease (NYSE:GSL) could be an interesting investment play. A seasoned investor recently suggested it as a compelling opportunity in the leasing space. In 2025, this is our first initiation of coverage. So, we report that the company is a UK-based container shipowner, leasing ships to container shipping players. Global Ship Lease focuses on smaller & mid-sized container ships, owning 72 vessels as of December 2024. Its clients include top-tier container liner companies such as CMA, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC, and Maersk (Fig 1).

Source: GSL Q4 Results Presentation - Fig 1

Cross-checking its Q4 results, we report that the company beat Wall Street analysts on higher top-line sales. GSL board announced a dividend of $0.45 per share, consistent with its past two payouts, and a supplemental dividend of $0.075 per share, for a total DPS of $2.10. The company added key sales to its order backlog, which reached $1.88 billion at year-end. With a Fiscal Year 2024 EPS of $9.99, GSL trades at a P/E of 2x. With a deleveraging process and a tactical buyback, this company is a buy. That said, we provide a positive view based on the following key takeaways.

Why are we positive?

  1. (Asset-Light Strategy for GSL clients). Following the “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, we might expect further volatility in the weeks ahead. With uncertain times on the horizon, we should report that the construction time for cargo vessels varies based on size, and shipyard capacity is approximately set at 3.2 years. This reflects a limited shipyard availability. Global Ship Lease allows top-tier container liner companies to immediately increase fleet size without waiting for newbuild deliveries or competing in secondhand sales markets. Chartering ships gives flexibility without locking in capital on ship purchases and helps these companies to scale up capacity quickly in response to demand (e.g., during surges in global trade) without the long lead time of new shipbuilding. Looking ahead, we expect new charters for GSL, allowing companies to lock in costs and avoid technical maintenance and drydock planning for those vessels;
  2. (Upside from Liquidation Value). In the last quarter, the company communicated the sale of three older container ships: the 2000-built Tasman (5,900 TEU), the 2002-built Akiteta (2,200 TEU), and the 2003-built Reta (2,200 TEU). This disposal has an expected value of $54.5 million compared to a book value of $24.9 million. The Akiteta was delivered to its new owners in February 2025, and the other two are expected to be delivered in H1 2025. Therefore, we see a clear upside in GSL's liquidation value. Additionally, the company communicated the acquisition of four eco-design 3,000 TEU ships. GSL's cash position reached $273.8 million, and with a total gross debt of $691.1 million, the company's net LTV stands at a healthy 33% (Fig 2);
  3. (Supportive OPEX strategy). Still related to the new Global Ship Lease OPEX plan, the company is playing well with the decarbonization strategy. The company is progressing with investment in energy efficiency, thus reducing the emissions of existing ships. GLS investments are not moving on in new energy efficiency vessels, thanks to the latest regulatory requirements. Still, they are moving on with technical enhancements and operational adjustments to improve the energy efficiency of existing ships by almost 20%. Considering the carbon footprint associated with construction and the GSL fleet, which is older than average, the company has a current hedge that takes a full life-cycle approach to its ship sustainability, considering the carbon footprint associated with construction, operation, and eventual recycling;
  4. (Solid execution and downside protection). The company has a meaningful revenue backlog that continues to grow. Since 2023's end, the company has added $884.8 million to its revenue backlog, bringing the total to $1.88 billion (Fig 1). In detail, GSL extended the charter contracts of the CMA Thalassa and GSL Alice by three years each. This includes daily rates of $47,200 and $31,000, respectively. Additionally, the company secured extensions for GSL Lydia, GSL Alexandra, GSL Effie, and GSL Sofia by one year each at their current rates. As already reported, at the time of writing, GLS trades (excluding special dividend in place) at a dividend yield of >10% with a buyback yield of 4.6% (Fig 3). This double-digit total return offers downside protection and is also combined with a successful deleveraging path.

Fig 2

Fig 3

Financials Estimate and Valuation

The company reported an adj. EPS of $2.55 in Q4, beating the Wall Street consensus estimates. Also, the adj. EBITDA reached $123.7 million, ahead of the consensus of $117.3 million. GSL earnings beats were attributable to higher sales, representing a realized daily TCE rate of $29,531. This TCE was the highest of the year, while the company's operating expenses have been consistent. GSL normalized Q4 FCF amounted to $1.90 per share, with an FCF yield of 31%. Given its order backlog, we project a full-year sale of $670 million, with slightly higher costs than the 2024 results. Our 2025 EBITDA moved from $494.7 to $460 million, but we report that the company is taking advantage of linear interest in tonnage. Therefore, we have higher visibility in the 2025 financial results, which is also combined with the expectation of FCF generation. Considering the current deleverage in progress, our interest rate expenses are $30 million in 2025, with a net income projection of $320 million and an EPS of $9.05. GSL trades at an FWD P/E of 2.23x with an EV/EBITDA of 2.5x. These multiples represent a significant discount, which is hard to justify. On a downside protection, as the company continues to de-lever, equity value builds steadily — and assuming valuation multiples remain constant, this translates directly into increased market valuation.

On a 12-month view, given the company's revenue backlog and earnings visibility, we apply a 3.6x P/E (Fig 4), arriving at a valuation of $32.58. Our P/E valuation is aligned with the company's FWD historical P/E average of 3.6x.

Fig 4

Risks

Downside risks include GSL's older fleet, even if management has a supportive approach to it. A significant portion of GSL's revenue comes from a limited number of clients, which raises some concerns. The company's top-line sales mainly depended on charter rates (Fig 6), which can be volatile. As already reported, the shipping industry is subject to stringent environmental regulations. Global events, such as trade wars, pandemics, cybersecurity attacks, or conflicts (Fig 5), can disrupt shipping routes and affect supply and demand for container shipping services. These disruptions can lead to lower profits.

Fig 5

Fig 6

Conclusion

We find Global Ship Lease shares compelling. They are trading well below its NAV despite the company’s earnings growth, strong balance sheet, and downside protection thanks to a double-digit yield.

data.title: JNK: Intensifying Spread Risk Has Yet To Be Priced By The Market
data.description:
In contrasting fashion to treasury bonds, the SPDR® Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:JNK) held up strongly in the past week, suggesting potential decoupling in the bond market. Unfortunately, we think the vehicle is bound for a drawdown, believing its recent market-based performance is detached from reality.

Here are the reasons why we would avoid investing in JNK ETF for now.

Fund Overview

JNK ETF is a tracker fund with the objective of providing investors with liquid access to the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index. The vehicle's primary exposure includes fixed-rate corporate bonds with occasional optionality. Moreover, as its name suggests, JNK ETF invests in non-investment grade debt, which explains its 411.38 option-adjusted spread.

Herewith is a compact view of the fund's salient variables. The chart is pivotal as its contents are referred to throughout the analysis.

A quality map illustrates that JNK ETF primarily invests in BA3, BA2, B2, B3, and BA1-rated corporate credit. The fund follows a liquidity-based weighting method, meaning its credit rating exposure might change as time passes. Sure, lower-rated bonds usually possess lower liquidity; however, other factors might alter the liquidity environment.

A sectoral overview indicates that JNK ETF is sector diversified. The ETF has elevated exposure to cyclical domains like cyclical consumer products, energy, and capital goods. However, its seasonal exposure is countered by non-cyclical sectoral exposure, namely communications, non-cyclical consumer goods, and insurance. Similar to its credit rating make-up, we could see a shift in sectoral exposure as time passes.

Time series data illustrates JNK ETF's excess return properties. Though it has delivered shaky price returns, JNK ETF's total returns have outpaced those of the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund ETF (BND).

While JNK ETF's relative returns deserve respect, we highlight its risk surplus. The ETF has a 5% monthly value-at-risk of 4.01%, displaying a significant discrepancy when compared to BND ETF. Additionally, JNK ETF posts an expected monthly shortfall of 8.48%, which we deem a staggering amount for a corporate bond ETF.

Note: Monthly VAR (5%) illustrates the maximum amount an asset has lost in 95% of its trading months. Expected Shortfall, measures the average of the monthly returns that exceeded the 95% confidence interval.

Market Commentary

Recent Performance

Treasuries sold sharply last week, shown by IEF ETF's (IEF) capitulation. Market participants were generally surprised by the event as some argued treasuries would spike in a crisis scenario. While treasuries sold, certain high-yield bonds help up, illustrated by JNK ETF's -0.03% five-day return.

A longer term view shows that U.S. high-yield corporate bonds have outperformed investment grade debt in the past three years, despite suffering price losses. Unless a moderate-to-hard economic landing occurs, we'll likely see this trend resuming.

Interest Rate & Credit Spread Duration

We've argued for weeks that the market will soon price a U.S. economic downturn, regardless of the trade war frictions that escalated last week. Yet, it has to be acknowledged that a potential slowdown in global trade might exacerbate matters, leading to a hard landing.

Considering our core outlook, we have a negative intermediate term view of the credit cycle. In addition, as discussed last week, we expect the market to price a recession in 2025, which would likely lead to inversion and downward levelling of the treasury yield curve. In tandem, we see additional spread widening being en route, a phenomenon we've predicted since mid-to-late last year.

Considering our systematic outlook, we believe bonds with intermediate-to-long term interest rate duration and short-term credit duration will outperform the broader asset class. Unfortunately, JNK ETF possesses an option-adjusted spread duration of circa 442 basis points, in-line with the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread. Simultaneously, the ETF has short-dated interest rate duration of 3.31 years paired with optionality, meaning it's a bet on the short-end of the par rate yield curve.

In essence, JNK ETF is everything we don't want at this time. Panel data shows JNK ETF's negative correlations with B-grade option-adjusted spreads, meaning JNK ETF will likely lose market value in the event that our macro outlook materialises.

Credit Migration

Risky bonds typically face three headline risk premiums, namely interest rate risk, spread risk, and credit migration. For those unaware, migration refers to rating changes, and the influence of rating transitions on bond prices. The effect can be docile for a sustained period as agencies update their consensus infrequently; however, agencies can be pro-cyclical, meaning deterioration of the credit cycle might trigger a series of downgrades.

High-yield expected default rates have remained relatively flat since mid-2023 but actual default rates spiked in 2024, possibly due to an underestimation of macro-level variables. Broad-based corporate defaults have surged to 9.2%, partly due to leveraged loan defaults. High-yield corporate debt has lagged the broad-based default level, yet we see high-yield corporate defaults catching speed in mid-to-late 2025 on the basis of our systematic argument.

Is it all doom and gloom for credit ratings? Not necessarily. While we hold a tentative view, we acknowledge that JNK is sector diversified. Economic tail risk can enhance sectoral credit risk correlation; however, we think benefit has to be given to sector diversified ETFs for now, considering the current divergence in sectoral earnings performance.

JNK'S Key Metrics

Yield

The SPDR® Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF has a yield to maturity of around 8.64% but we deem the figure somewhat redundant as JNK ETF doesn't run down the clock on its holdings. Thus, we think distribution and premium (discount)-to-NAV should be isolated in a returns-based analysis of the vehicle.

JNK has a distribution yield of 6.90%, which we deem stellar. For illustrative purposes, we highlight that the ETF's distribution yield is about 85 basis points higher than iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG)'s trailing yield.

Premium (Discount)/NAV

JNK ETF trades at a premium-to-NAV, which doesn't seem like a massive risk if we look at past premiums (discounts)/NAV. That said, macro headwinds can drain bond market liquidity, in turn exposing JNK ETF's premium as a key risk factor.

Risk-Return

I mentioned the ETF's value-at-risk and expected shortfall earlier in the article but decided to discuss its maximum drawdown in this section for structural purposes. As shown by the diagram, JNK ETF experienced a maximum drawdown of around 38.51% during the great financial crisis. The odds of a reoccurrence remains slim, yet a moderate drawdown could erode the ETF's yield benefits. Considering our negative macro-level outlook, we reiterate the risks tied to JNK ETF.

Conclusion

We see challenges emerging in the credit cycle, largely due to cyclical economic growth and the added catalyst of slowing implied global trade. JNK ETF presents a compelling yield. Nonetheless, we think JNK might face drawdown risk in mid-to-late 2025, and therefore decided to argue against a bullish scenario.

Consensus: Market Underperform/Avoid

data.title: Booking Holdings: The One Stop Shop Of The Tourism Sector
data.description:
This is the first time I’m writing about Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG), and it will be a stock I’ll start covering here on Seeking Alpha. Booking is the largest player in the travel and tourism industry when it comes to rentals—hotels, apartments, trips, etc. Founded in 1997, it has gone through several changes in its history. The CEO has been with the company since 2000 and is a true expert in the sector. I find it a very interesting stock to gain exposure to the macro trends of tourism, travel, and experiences—but through an asset-light company with growth above the sector average.

Business Model

The parent company, Booking Holdings, is made up of several businesses that serve different verticals within the travel ecosystem—an industry growing at a steady 3%-4% annually:

  • Booking: Hotel and apartment bookings... now also including flights.

  • Price Line: A site offering discounts on hotels, flights, and rental cars, with a focus on last-minute deals.

  • Kayak: A travel search engine that compares prices for flights, hotels, and rental cars across various websites.

  • Agoda: A booking platform particularly strong in Asia, offering hotels, flights, and activities.

  • Rentalcars: A service specializing in rental car comparison and booking worldwide.

  • OpenTable: A platform to book restaurant tables, browse menus, and read customer reviews.

I like it because, much like we saw in the luxury sector (though the comparison has its limits), Booking is focusing on the full spectrum of experiences—creating an all-in-one solution within a single platform. This allows them to share existing users across different services, further boosting their network effects. A true “one-stop option” in the travel and tourism sector, with an asset-light model that should hold up better when times get tough.

Business Segments

  • Agency Revenues: This refers to a commission model—meaning, the customer pays at the hotel, and the hotel later sends Booking its share.

  • Merchant Revenues: This applies when the customer pays at the time of booking (Booking then forwards the payment to the hotel, keeping a commission). They’ve implemented their own internal payment system—which didn’t exist before—now offering discounts, insurance, and more.

  • Advertising and Other Revenues: This includes revenue generated through advertising on some of their platforms like KAYAK and OpenTable. These function as directories leading to OTAs (Online Travel Agencies)—portals that redirect users to booking platforms. For example, when you browse hotels on Trivago, it aggregates offers from many platforms, and clicking on one redirects you to Booking. This is exactly what Google does. Every time a user clicks and gets redirected, the OTA (like Booking) pays a small fee to the referring site.

One could imagine why Merchant revenue has recently overtaken Agency revenue so significantly. Booking now offers discounts through its Merchant model, which can make it more attractive to customers. Additionally, this setup allows the company to collect cash upfront, which has notably increased accounts payable—and in turn, free cash flow. This could mean FCF is temporarily inflated in an "artificial" way.

Booking purchases hotel rooms in advance (thanks to its scale) at a discounted rate and then resells them to customers at the regular price (or slightly lower). The company keeps the difference as profit. Although hotels receive slightly less money, they get paid earlier, which improves their cash flow conversion.

Genius Loyalty Program: I think Genius is a smart way to boost revenue per customer by making them more loyal, more valuable, and cheaper to retain. It also reduces dependence on acquiring new customers through paid advertising, which is usually more expensive. On top of that, it drives increased usage of the mobile app, cutting out intermediaries and allowing Booking to capture a higher margin on each transaction.

Competitive Advantages

As I mentioned in one of my articles on Fortinet, I’m going to use a similar analogy here with Booking.

I see Booking as a kind of Mr. Potato Head, to which you can keep adding new pieces in the form of different platforms that create strong synergies with one another. They're constantly adding new solutions to the ecosystem: flights, dining, payments... all integrated into a single, unified platform. The value proposition keeps growing, driving both data generation and reinforcing network effects on both sides of the marketplace: supply (more available listings) and demand (more users and increased loyalty).

To me, it’s a highly scalable business that can easily branch out into new verticals, not just hotels and apartments, but also cars, trips, flights, Airbnbs... basically everything vacation-related. The end goal is to become a true one-stop shop for travel.

On top of that, with over $6B in annual free cash flow, just 2% of CapEx, and negative working capital, they have the financial strength to make key acquisitions when necessary. It might not look like a market with high entry barriers, but Booking’s competitive advantages, mainly its network effects, are solid and continue to strengthen over time.

What’s more, I see strong upside optionality in the implementation of AI for chat features, travel planning, or even search optimization. If we assume that the cost of AI training and iteration will continue to drop significantly with developments like DeepSeek, Booking is in a privileged position to benefit, as they already have the other two parts of the equation: the largest proprietary dataset in the world and the largest global travel distribution network.

The CEO has addressed this in several interviews and podcasts I’ve listened to. Ultimately, I imagine Booking could build something like a “Copilot” where, even through voice commands, you could guide the platform to plan your ideal trip powered by a mix of user platform data and your own personal consumption habits.

Geographical Dynamics

Despite being listed in the U.S. and reporting its revenues in dollars, only around 10% of Booking’s sales actually come from the United States. This is largely due to the different market structures between the U.S. and regions like Europe.

The high number of independent hotels in Europe actually plays in Booking’s favor, as these smaller players don’t have the same bargaining power as the large franchises that dominate the U.S. market. At the end of the day, Booking is also competing against direct bookings from these large hotel chains. As long as the European market remains fragmented, Booking is likely to perform better there than in the U.S.

This is actually the main reason, one I hadn’t fully understood before, why Booking has struggled to gain meaningful traction in the U.S. market. In the U.S., around 70% of hotels (and even flights with airlines) are tied to major franchises like Hilton, Marriott, or American Airlines. These players not only have the ability to build their own direct booking and loyalty systems, but they also form tight partnerships with companies like American Express, creating even more formidable barriers to entry.

These are structural dynamics that don’t seem likely to change anytime soon.

Capital Allocation

Booking’s capital allocation is excellent. The vast majority of its free cash flow has been directed toward share buybacks—in fact, the company has retired 29% of its outstanding shares over the past 7 years.

Another important milestone is that this year, Booking started paying dividends. While the yield is modest—just 0.8%—it reflects a 20% payout ratio. In any case, I see this as a positive development. It’s an efficient way to return capital to shareholders, especially when the stock is trading at higher valuations and buybacks may not be as effective.

If the total capital returned to shareholders exceeds 100% of the free cash flow generated, it’s because the difference has been financed with debt. And even so, the company still maintains a net cash position of $1.3B.

On the other hand, it's worth highlighting that despite being a tech company, stock-based compensation represents only 3% of revenue and 7% of operating cash flow. Regarding SBC, management made a comment that should delight shareholders in the Q4 2022 conference call.

"We continue to see many publicly traded companies pro forma out the very real expense associated with stock-based compensation. We strongly disagree with this approach, and therefore, every profit metric we report includes the negative impact of stock-based compensation expense.

We view SBC expense as a very real cost of doing business across every stakeholder should fully count when evaluating the performance and returns of our business or any business. If anything, we view SBC dollars even more valuable than cash dollars because of our long-term expectation that dollars worth of stock to-date will be worth more in the future. It’s the same expectation that serves as the rationale for pursuing our share repurchase program, a program that has meaningfully reduced our share count over time has not just served to offset dilution from SBC.

Simply offsetting dilution does not represent a return on capital to shareholders, but actually represents a cash drain on our business that does not get counted in many companies’ pro forma reporting of profits. In 2022, our stock-based compensation resulted in less than 0.7% of shareholder dilution. And during the last 5 years, it resulted in less than 3% of cumulative dilution. As I mentioned, during the same period, we reduced total share count by a net 22%, inclusive of the shares that were added as a result of our stock-based compensation activities. Our future practices will continue to be guided by the same two philosophical approaches that guide us decades, namely, number one, the stock-based compensation counts; and two, that our stock repurchases, first and foremost, are meant – are actively meant to return capital to shareholders in the form of share count reductions." David Goulden, CEO.

Financials

Booking has very solid fundamentals. Growth rates are in the high single digits, although it’s true that the company hasn’t yet returned to its pre-pandemic margin levels. This is partly due to the shift toward a Merchant-based revenue model rather than the traditional Agency model (as previously explained). As a result, we’ve seen some operational deleveraging.

However, thanks to a 29% reduction in shares outstanding over the past seven years, EPS has grown at a 12.93% CAGR, compared to just 6.65% for net income. That share buyback strategy has clearly helped enhance shareholder value, even during a period of margin adjustment.

If we look at its free cash flow, this has been the fastest-growing figure. As I mentioned earlier, the transition toward the Merchant model has also contributed to stronger cash conversion. In fact, accounts payable have increased significantly, which may be temporarily inflating FCF over the past few years—until we reach more normalized levels.

Once again, we can see the impact of share buybacks here: while FCF has grown at 8.1%, FCF per share has increased at a much faster rate of 14.5%. I should also note, and this ties into a recent management comment on stock-based compensation, that I’ve adjusted the FCF figures to account for SBC as well.

This same effect is what makes Booking’s working capital highly negative, effectively eliminating the need for debt financing. In fact, the company currently holds a net cash position of $1.3B. This strongly negative working capital significantly reduces the denominator in the ROIC (invested capital) calculation—resulting in those extremely high ROIC figures, around 66%.

Valuation

I’ll keep this section brief. I’m going to run a reverse DCF to try and estimate the free cash flow growth rate the market is currently pricing into the stock—using a minimum required IRR of 10% and a terminal growth rate of 3%.

At the time of writing, the stock is trading at around $4,600, which implies a P/E ratio of 22x and an EV/FCF of 20x when adjusted for SBC. These multiples seem fair given the quality of the underlying business. Based on the reverse DCF model mentioned earlier, the market appears to be pricing in a free cash flow growth rate of around 7.5%.

If we add to that a 5% “yield” implied by the 20x multiple (i.e., the earnings yield), we could be looking at a total return potential of roughly 12.5% annually over the coming years. For a company of this caliber, those are quite attractive expected returns. For all these reasons, I rate the stock as a Buy.

Risks

It seems that regulations are starting to play a role. In fact, in markets like the UK, regulators have already blocked certain acquisitions. However, rather than being a direct risk to the company, this acts more as a limitation to continued inorganic growth.

There’s competition across different niches, some of them more specialized, but given Booking’s position as a one-stop shop with simple and effective tech integration, I believe it can continue to lead the space. I also believe that companies like Airbnb's (NASDAQ:ABNB) should be more concerned about Booking than the other way around. In the end, software companies with strong network effects often develop “winner takes all” dynamics. I’m not saying that’s necessarily going to happen, but Booking does have a larger user base and a more comprehensive value proposition—factors that could eventually push Airbnb out of its niche in the segment.

Consumer Confidence. At least in my opinion, travel and experiences are fairly discretionary expenses that heavily depend on consumer confidence. It’s worth noting that this data comes from the U.S. consumer, which—as we've discussed—is not Booking’s most important region due to the structural dynamics of that geography. Still, U.S. consumer sentiment is currently at a 10-year low, according to the University of Michigan.

Conclusion

Booking has been a company I’ve truly enjoyed learning about, which is why I plan to continue covering it here on Seeking Alpha. Overall, it checks all the boxes of what I look for: a dominant market position, a favorable long-term industry trend, strong competitive advantages, solid management, and healthy fundamentals.

And since it also appears to be trading at a reasonable valuation, that’s why I’m giving it a Buy rating.

data.title: Arbor Realty Trust: My Mistake, Slower Growth Outlook
data.description:
Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) is one of my more-covered tickers on Seeking Alpha. I initially rated it a Buy in the fall of 2023, eventually downgraded to Hold, and finally gave it a Buy again last September.

The last six months, shares are down 30%. This time, it isn't due to short reports casting shade. Rather, it's due to realized weaknesses on their loan book and the slower growth outlook ahead. Consequently, I am downgrading once more to Hold, and I am here to explain why.

Summary of Previous Thesis

My previous thesis, in which I restored my Buy rating, hinged on one important detail at the time: the 10Y Note's yield fell below 4%.

The period marked in the green box above shows that the yield had been that low for over a month, and it seemed likely to persist. This was important because the main hurdle impacting their balance sheet was the maturation of bridge loans originated in the 2021 real estate boom that needed to be refinanced into long-term mortgages.

To remind folks of the business model that supports Arbor's dividend, it comes down to three main components:

  1. Net interest income from bridge loans and similar investments
  2. Servicing fees from their Agency business, which they use to refinance those bridge loans
  3. Interest from their cash and escrow balances

The second one was the key, as the loan becomes an Agency asset that no longer sits on Arbor's book. It restores their principal, allowing them to originate new loans and continue the cycle. If rates are higher, this depresses property values and makes refinancing for the original amount of principal harder.

As many of these 2021-era loans were issued while the Fed cut rates to near-zero, the rapid rate increase that followed in 2022 made it harder for borrowers to refinance.

Seen above from my thesis last August, the material impact was clear. The amount of loans (among the multifamily portfolio) that fell below the "Pass/Watch" rating grew between Q1 and Q2, for example. A declining 10Y yield was therefore a welcome sign that many of these loans could be successfully refinanced. CEO Ivan Kaufman made this point himself back in the Q4 2023 earnings call:

But if the 10-year goes below 4% again, it will become more meaningful and every quarter of a point drop in rates from here will be even more impactful.

As management had managed to refinance, restructure, or renegotiate many of the maturing loans up to that point, keep a large cash balance, and avoid cutting the dividend up to that point, I believed the outlook looked positive once again.

Updates Since Then

Of course, we know now that 10Y Note did not fall in further increments of 25 basis points. It did not even remain below 4% for much longer after my last thesis. Kaufman therefore addressed this fairly early in the Q4 2024 earnings call:

However, rates have not just remained elevated, they have actually increased significantly with the 10 year rising from 360 in September to as high as 480 in January and now is hovering around 450 with the current outlook suggesting it will remain at these levels for the near-term.

This is a material change in the market resulting in significant headwinds that will affect everybody in the space. These elevated rates are creating a very challenging environment as it relates to agency origination volumes and where we've experienced success over the last few years in getting borrowers to the transition of fixed rate loans and recap their deals, we expect this environment will create a deceleration in this area as well.

These stressors showed themselves over the course of 2024 with assets clearly in runoff. Slower originations led to most asset categories declining. Exceptions were the ones that proved the trend with real estate owned doubling (real estate they repossessed from defaulting borrowers), and "other assets" growing largely to reflect the rise in their modified loans.

The good news is that Arbor was prudent to reduce its leverage and minimize its risk as it navigated these hurdles, and 2024 was not a year when assets declined, while liabilities remained.

While rising rates increased their interest income in 2023, we can see that it fell below 2022's levels once the loans started coming due and experienced distress. Total other revenue had a more slight decline, pointing to the strength their Agency segment provides, and it was still above 2022 levels.

Seen above, the multifamily portfolio, which makes up the vast majority of the loan book, ended 2024 with similar levels of assets rated Special Mention or lower, indicating that not all of this was behind them. Worth nothing, assets with these ratings shifted primarily from the 2020 and 2021 vintages into 2022. It's not great, but it does show that Arbor managed to rotate and work with some of these distressed loans, buying time.

Future Outlook

The biggest near-term factor to consider is the dividend. Reflecting on how reality diverged from his own expectations, Kaufman noted the following:

Based on these factors, we are now estimating our earnings for 2025 will be in the range of $0.30 to $0.35 a quarter and will likely reset our dividend starting in the first quarter of this year in accordance with this new guidance.

As quarterly dividends over the past couple of years had been $0.43 per share, this suggests that cut of at least 30% is likely. As my previous valuation had assumed the dividend would be maintained, any assertion of undervaluation I had made up to that point was incorrect. Going forward, I think tangible book value per share is going to be a better gauge of value until Arbor's situation stabilizes.

As of Q4, TBV per share comes to $12.16. Recent prices under $11 offer a discount there, but as stress remains on the book, there is room for further decline of TBV. The current prices also reset the yield to about 11%, should the dividend fall to that level of $0.30 per quarter.

As the pain isn't over, I don't think it's prudent to say ABR is a Buy, and the lower price at least reflects a more accurate outlook if the balance sheet contracts further. For that reason, I return to my Hold rating. I don't think Arbor is going out of business, but my Buy thesis can no longer stand until enough positives emerge in 2025.

Conclusion

What made ABR tempting was that its business was designed to give multiple outs. The focus on workforce multifamily, safer among the CRE loans, as well as the Agency business, put them in a better position than many other mREITs. Similarly, their CLO financing gave them the flexibility that would allow them to navigate a period of distress. Ultimately, these strengths were mitigated by the glut of 2021 mortgage environment.

Seen above, Arbor sold a lot of shares in 2021 and 2022, in order to raise capital for these originations, something they were never forced to do. They could have worked with what they had and focused on the loans that were most likely to prevail.

The lesson? Lending is cyclical, and no lender is too good to be invulnerable to cyclical factors, especially if they do too much business. Going forward, I will be more skeptical of lending sprees like those seen in 2021, even among the better lenders. Until Arbor finishes getting its house in order from those 2021 and 2022 vintages, I'm returning to a Hold rating, which, I think, makes sense for the current price.
